ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'milk tea' class (FOODON_03301099); no specific caramel milk tea entry exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers tea-based beverages with milk; no caramel-specific sub-code exists. Closest parent match documented.

allergens: milk (non-dairy creamer may contain milk derivatives)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 ml (one cup), equivalent to 250 g, as commonly served at Singaporean bubble tea and speciality tea outlets.

nutrient_source.title: Caramel Milk Tea (No Topping) — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Caramel Milk Tea (No Topping), cr_id D08010014, with confidence 0.95. All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for sweetened milk tea / sugar-sweetened tea beverages
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for caramel milk tea in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous sugar-sweetened milk tea entries in the Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables, which report GI values of 60–70 for sweetened tea beverages with milk and added sugar. Caramel syrup contributes sucrose (GI ~65); non-dairy creamer fat slightly attenuates peak glucose response. GL per serving calculated as: GI (65) × carbohydrate per serving (27.65 g from 250 g × 11.06 g/100g) / 100 = 17.97, rounded to 17.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (11.06 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and blood pressure elevation; sodium is low at 13 mg/100 g but does not offset the sugar-driven BP risk.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid this beverage; the 11.06 g/100 g sugar load in liquid form independently raises blood pressure risk (AHA 2018) and should be replaced with unsweetened black tea or water.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 11.06 g/100 g (all carbohydrate is sugar) with zero dietary fibre in a liquid matrix causes rapid glucose absorption; estimated GI of 65 and GL of 17.9 per 250 ml serving represent a significant glycaemic load.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage entirely; the high sugar-to-fibre ratio and liquid delivery maximise postprandial glucose excursion, and a zero-sugar or unsweetened preparation should be requested instead.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Fat content of 2.41 g/100 g is likely predominantly saturated fat from non-dairy creamer (palm oil base); combined with 11.06 g/100 g added sugar, this beverage contributes to dyslipidaemia and cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this beverage; the probable saturated fat from non-dairy creamer and high added-sugar content adversely affect lipid profiles and should be replaced with unsweetened black tea.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined sugar at 11.06 g/100 g promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine production; non-dairy creamer likely contains palm oil (pro-inflammatory saturated fat); zero dietary fibre and no anti-inflammatory micronutrients.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage and replace it with unsweetened black tea, which provides theaflavin polyphenols with documented anti-inflammatory properties.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Caramel Milk Tea (No Topping) is a freshly prepared sugar-sweetened beverage popular in Singapore's speciality tea and bubble tea outlets. It is made by combining brewed black tea with non-dairy creamer and caramel syrup, producing a sweet, creamy, amber-coloured drink with a smooth texture and a pronounced caramel-toffee flavour. The 'no topping' designation indicates the drink is served without add-ins such as tapioca pearls, grass jelly, or pudding. Per 100 g, it provides 65 kcal, 0.33 g protein, 2.41 g fat, 11.06 g carbohydrate (entirely as sugar), 0 g dietary fibre, and 13 mg sodium. The beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) due to its 11.06 g/100 g sugar content, which exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold. Clinically, it poses a high glycaemic concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes (estimated GI 65, GL 17.9 per 250 ml serving), a high cardiovascular concern due to probable saturated fat from non-dairy creamer, and a high hypertension concern due to sugar-driven insulin-mediated sodium retention. It is not suitable for individuals managing diabetes, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis. The drink is widely consumed as a breakfast or afternoon beverage in Singapore and is available across hawker centres, food courts, and speciality tea chains.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D08010014) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singaporean foods. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate (medium confidence component) but is documented with an approved GI source reference.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source; this is consistent with a processed beverage containing no plant fibre components. 2. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this caramel milk tea preparation is not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry D08010014 as of 2026-04-16. 3. potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this beverage as of 2026-04-16; no approved secondary source provides a sufficiently close match to justify estimation. 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for sugar-sweetened milk tea beverages and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for caramel milk tea exists in the approved GI database. 5. Halal status assumed based on typical Singaporean speciality tea preparation; kosher status not confirmed. 6. Non-dairy creamer ingredient composition (palm oil vs. hydrogenated vegetable oil) varies by vendor; saturated fat content may be higher than the -1 sentinel suggests.
